source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
Compiling
models into a single, self-contained binary or library can significantly simplify cross-platform deployment.

However, as these
binaries encapsulate key architectural details and scheduling
logic, adversaries seeking to reverse-engineer the DNN gain
additional attack surfaces [4], [6], as shown in Fig. 1.

Once
compiled, the code essentially becomes an artifact containing
valuable insights into operator shapes, memory layouts, and
potential hyperparameters.

B.

Model Extraction Attacks
Model extraction attacks aim to replicate or partially reconstruct a proprietary DNN by observing its behavior or analyzing
IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON DEPENDABLE AND SECURE COMPUTING, VOL. 22, NO. 6, NOVEMBER/DECEMBER 2025
artifacts such as executables or side-channel signals [14].

Early
research on these attacks focused on simple black-box scenarios,
where adversaries query the model’s input-output pairs to train
a ‘shadow’ network [15].

However, more recent methods delve
into the compilation and execution process.
1) Symbolic Execution & Disassembly: By disassembling
the compiled binary and applying symbolic execution, attackers
cantrackhowdataﬂowsthroughloopsandconditionalbranches.

DND [5] and BTD [6] rely on partial or selective instrumentation
to identify operator boundaries, gather weight shapes, or de-
duce kernel dimensions.

These methods exploit instruction-level
semantics and symbolic path constraints to recover operator
sequences, even in stripped or statically linked binaries.
2) Side-Channel Analysis: Other adversaries collect execution traces such as cache hits/misses [16] or performance counters [17], enabling them to infer operator boundaries or compute
intensities.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
DND [5] and BTD [6] rely on partial or selective instrumentation
to identify operator boundaries, gather weight shapes, or de-
duce kernel dimensions.

These methods exploit instruction-level
semantics and symbolic path constraints to recover operator
sequences, even in stripped or statically linked binaries.
2) Side-Channel Analysis: Other adversaries collect execution traces such as cache hits/misses [16] or performance counters [17], enabling them to infer operator boundaries or compute
intensities.

Hu et al. [4] proposed a model extraction framework
that reverse-engineers DNN architectures by establishing em-
pirical connections between operational traces (e.g., read/write
magnitudesextractedfrommemoryside-channels)andthelatent
architectural properties of target DNN models.

Such attacks
leverage ﬁne-grained temporal or spatial access patterns to correlate hardware-level leakage with high-level operator behavior,
often without needing code visibility.
3) Learning-Based Reconstructions: Recent studies have
explored DL-based model extraction attacks, learning the contextual relationship of operators to infer DNN architectures and
parameters.

Li et al. [7] combines code representation learning
method and binary analysis to enhance reverse engineering
accuracy against compiled deep learning models.

C.

Model Obfuscation
Model obfuscation is a critical technique to defend against
neural network architecture reverse engineering, ensuring the
conﬁdentiality of model structures, hyperparameters, and computational graphs [18], [19].

Existing works have explored
different strategies to mitigate such threats.

Luo et al.
proposed the framework through introducing scheduling transformations that alter the execution order and memory access
patterns of tensor computations without compromising model
correctness.

Huang et al. [8] introduced the method that al-
lows model providers to selectively conﬁgure which layers to
protect.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
Model Obfuscation
Model obfuscation is a critical technique to defend against
neural network architecture reverse engineering, ensuring the
conﬁdentiality of model structures, hyperparameters, and computational graphs [18], [19].

Existing works have explored
different strategies to mitigate such threats.

Luo et al.
proposed the framework through introducing scheduling transformations that alter the execution order and memory access
patterns of tensor computations without compromising model
correctness.

Huang et al. [8] introduced the method that al-
lows model providers to selectively conﬁgure which layers to
protect.

During the compilation phase, the model provider can
specify particular layers that require protection.

SecureTVM
maps the computations of these protected layers to a secure
execution domain, while non-protected layers continue to run
in the standard domain.

However, selective protection means
that certain parts of the model might still be vulnerable to
reverse engineering attacks if not properly secured.

The tradeoff between privacy and computational efﬁciency remains a
challenge.

Beyond compiler-based defenses, prior works have explored
model watermarking [20], pruning-based obfuscation [21], and
encryption-based protections [22].

These methods typically fo-
cus on ownership veriﬁcation, model size reduction, or parameter conﬁdentiality, can not resit the novel attack mentioned in the
previous section.

In contrast, MACO targets structural obfuscation at the IR level, aiming to disrupt architecture-level reverse
engineering while preserving full inference functionality.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
In this section, we introduce the design principles of MACO.

The overall architecture is divided into three modules: the op-
erator layer, memory layer, and graph layer.

Each designed to
obfuscate a different attacker perspective.

Fig. 2 illustrates an
overview of the MACO architecture.

The upper portion depicts
the normal compilation ﬂow, while the lower portion highlights
MACO’s obfuscation pathway.

At the two principal IR stages,
MACO inserts different obfuscation strategies, which are de-
tailed in the following sections.

However, before delving into
these strategies, we ﬁrst articulate the threat model that MACO
is designed to defend against.

A.

Threat Model
We assume an adversary who has full access to the compiled
binary (TVM) of a deep learning model, including the ability
to disassemble the executable, analyze low-level intermediate
representations (e.g., LLVM IR or assembly), and apply symbolic execution or static data-ﬂow analysis.

The attacker is also
assumed to have proﬁling capabilities, such as access to performance counters or cache behavior via side-channel monitoring.

However, we do not assume access to the original model source
code (e.g., PyTorch or TensorFlow deﬁnitions), training data, or
runtime input/output pairs.

The adversary’s goal is to reconstruct
the model’s architecture, operator attributes, or computational
topology based on binary-level signals.

Query-Based extraction
techniques (e.g., API probing or input-output imitation) are out
of scope.

B.

Operator Attributes Obfuscation Set
For reverse extraction attacks, a crucial step in reconstructing
the model is inferring or computing operator attributes.

To
impede this process, we introduce mathematically equivalent
redundancies into operators.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
The adversary’s goal is to reconstruct
the model’s architecture, operator attributes, or computational
topology based on binary-level signals.

Query-Based extraction
techniques (e.g., API probing or input-output imitation) are out
of scope.

B.

Operator Attributes Obfuscation Set
For reverse extraction attacks, a crucial step in reconstructing
the model is inferring or computing operator attributes.

To
impede this process, we introduce mathematically equivalent
redundancies into operators.

Speciﬁcally, we deﬁne six obfuscation techniques within the obfuscation set.
1) Dimensional Proliferation (DP): It enhances model security by expanding the dimensionality of the weight tensor, introducing controlled redundancy without altering the output of the
operator.

This transformation complicates reverse-engineering
efforts by modifying the internal model structure, increasing the
number of output channels while preserving functional equivalence.

Consider a convolutional operator with weight tensor
W ∈Rk1×k2×c×j, where k1 and k2 are the kernel dimensions,
c is the number of input channels, and j is the number of
output channels.

3) Computational Obfuscation (CO): It introduces redundant yet numerically neutral operations into the network, such
as identity multiplications, zero additions, or padding.

This ad-
ditional computation increases the number of tensor operations,
thereby obscuring performance traces while leaving the ﬁnal
output unchanged.

For example, we have an original operation
deﬁned as Y = f(X), where f is an operator function and X is
the input.

In the CO transformation, a redundant term D(X) is
incorporated so that the output becomes Y ′ = f(X) + D(X).

Here, D(X) is designed to be neutral (e.g., zeros_like(X)
or an identity mapping InXIT
m, with In and Im as appropriately
sized identity matrices), ensuring that Y ′ = f(X) in effect, as
shown in Fig. 3(c).

Although these redundant operations do
not alter the functional output, they modify the computational
graph and memory access patterns, thus complicating reverseengineering efforts.
4) Hierarchical Augmentation (HA): It increases the effective depth of a neural network by inserting identity transformations between existing operators.

While these inserted

F(X), while the augmented computation graph now contains

C.

Tensor-Level Memory Layout Obfuscation
Beyond operator attribute modiﬁcations, we introduce tensor
layout transformations to perturb memory access patterns.

At-
tackers relying on side-channel signals, such as cache hits and
DRAM transactions, will be misled by our dynamic memory
MAPO module aims to obfuscate the memory access patterns
by manipulating the order in which tensor data is accessed.

By reordering tensor accesses or introducing random access
patterns, the model’s memory layout becomes more difﬁcult to
predict.

In a typical tensor operation, data is accessed in a sequential
or predictable manner, which could allow attackers to infer the
layout of the tensor in memory.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
At-
tackers relying on side-channel signals, such as cache hits and
DRAM transactions, will be misled by our dynamic memory
MAPO module aims to obfuscate the memory access patterns
by manipulating the order in which tensor data is accessed.

By reordering tensor accesses or introducing random access
patterns, the model’s memory layout becomes more difﬁcult to
predict.

In a typical tensor operation, data is accessed in a sequential
or predictable manner, which could allow attackers to infer the
layout of the tensor in memory.

The key idea behind MAPO
is to dynamically alter the order in which tensor elements are
accessed during computation.

This can be achieved by permuting the indices used to access the tensor or introducing random
access patterns based on a predeﬁned or dynamic scheme.

For instance, consider a tensor T with shape Rn1×n2×···×nk,
where n1, n2, . . . , nk are the dimensions of the tensor.

Instead
of accessing the elements of T in a straightforward sequence, we
introduce a permutation P that randomizes the access order by
applying a permutation function to the tensor indices.

Speciﬁ-
cally, the access pattern is altered at runtime such that the tensor
elements are accessed in a randomized order, effectively making
the original access sequence unpredictable.

This perturbation
in the access order ensures that the tensor’s data is scattered
across memory in a way that prevents attackers from inferring
the tensor layout based on predictable memory access patterns.

content:
Instead
of accessing the elements of T in a straightforward sequence, we
introduce a permutation P that randomizes the access order by
applying a permutation function to the tensor indices.

Speciﬁ-
cally, the access pattern is altered at runtime such that the tensor
elements are accessed in a randomized order, effectively making
the original access sequence unpredictable.

This perturbation
in the access order ensures that the tensor’s data is scattered
across memory in a way that prevents attackers from inferring
the tensor layout based on predictable memory access patterns.

This transformation still does not affect the model’s output,
but provides an additional obfuscation, making attempts to
reverse-engineer the memory organization through side-channel
analysis (such as cache proﬁling or DRAM transaction tracking)
more complicated.
2) Cache and Alignment Perturbation (CAP): The CAP
module introduces variability in memory access patterns by
altering memory alignment or cache utilization strategies.

By
adjusting how data is aligned in memory or how it is accessed
from the cache, CAP makes it signiﬁcantly more difﬁcult for
attackers to infer the data structure by analyzing cache hits and
misses.

During the compilation and inference execution process, data
is often aligned in memory according to a ﬁxed scheme that
optimizes cache efﬁciency.

Attackers can exploit this predictable
alignment to infer the structure of tensors.

If the variability
in alignment can be introduced, it would effectively make the
memory access patterns more difﬁcult to trace.

One way to achieve this is by altering the memory alignment
of the tensors during allocation.

Instead of aligning the tensor
data at a ﬁxed boundary, we introduce a random alignment
factor α, which serves as a random offset applied to the starting
memory address of tensor T.

paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
If the variability
in alignment can be introduced, it would effectively make the
memory access patterns more difﬁcult to trace.

One way to achieve this is by altering the memory alignment
of the tensors during allocation.

Instead of aligning the tensor
data at a ﬁxed boundary, we introduce a random alignment
factor α, which serves as a random offset applied to the starting
memory address of tensor T.

This random offset causes the
tensor to be placed at non-standard memory locations, disrupting
potential analysis based on ﬁxed memory alignment.

We can
dynamically adjust the alignment factor α at runtime.

This
effectively reduces the predictability of memory access patterns
and increases the difﬁculty of inferring the underlying memory
structure of the inference model.

D.

Computational Redundancy Expansion (CRE)
The CRE technique deliberately embeds redundant computations into the neural network’s execution graph without altering
its ﬁnal outputs, thereby complicating reverse-engineering ef-
forts.

CRE achieves this via two complementary strategies.

First,
from the overall graph structure perspective, fake control ﬂows
are introduced at the relay IR level using conditional branches
(e.g., if constructs).

In this approach, redundant neural network
computations are conditionally activated based on runtime pa-
rameters.

For example, consider a primary computation f(X)
for input X.

A redundant computation R(X) is conditionally
executed according to a runtime-dependent condition C(X),
and the augmented computation is deﬁned as
f ′(X) = f(X) + if
where ˜R(X) is a mathematically neutral operation (e.g., a se-
quence of arithmetic operations that cancel out) ensuring that
f ′(X) remains equivalent to f(X).

We illustrate the principle
more clearly in Fig. 3(g).

paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
A redundant computation R(X) is conditionally
executed according to a runtime-dependent condition C(X),
and the augmented computation is deﬁned as
f ′(X) = f(X) + if
where ˜R(X) is a mathematically neutral operation (e.g., a se-
quence of arithmetic operations that cancel out) ensuring that
f ′(X) remains equivalent to f(X).

We illustrate the principle
more clearly in Fig. 3(g).

Although these conditional branches
are executed at runtime (and thus affect scheduling and memory
access patterns), their results are designed to cancel out and
do not feed back into the main computation.

Second, at the
compilation level, particularly during the TIR lowering process,
dummy operators and dedicated dummy paths are inserted into
the computation graph.

These dummy paths are implemented
as true dummy operators whose outputs are mapped or scaled
to an insigniﬁcantly small range before being merged back
into the main path.

Moreover, dynamic indexing is introduced
within these dummy paths through runtime-dependent index
computations (such as random or unrelated index calculations).

This dynamic indexing makes the memory access patterns in
the dummy paths non-deterministic, thereby disrupting static
analysis of path dependencies.

Despite being computed during
inference, the output of these dummy paths is deliberately de-
signed to have no effect on the ﬁnal result.

In summary, CRE embeds both conditional and dummy operations into the execution graph.

These redundant computations
are conditionally activated and dynamically indexed so that they
perturb scheduling, memory access, and static analysis, while
their mathematical contributions cancel out, ensuring that the
main computational output remains unchanged.

Despite being computed during
inference, the output of these dummy paths is deliberately de-
signed to have no effect on the ﬁnal result.

In summary, CRE embeds both conditional and dummy operations into the execution graph.

These redundant computations
are conditionally activated and dynamically indexed so that they
perturb scheduling, memory access, and static analysis, while
their mathematical contributions cancel out, ensuring that the
main computational output remains unchanged.

Collectively, the three obfuscation transformations, derived
from different levels and perspectives, are seamlessly integrated
into the TVM compilation pipeline, ensuring that the obfuscated
model retains its original accuracy while posing signiﬁcant challenges for model extraction and reverse-engineering attacks.

In
the subsequent section, we detail the implementation strategies
that realize these transformations within TVM.

paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
We implement MACO as a modular transformation framework within TVM, leveraging its relay and TIR layers to
systematically inject obfuscation throughout the compilation

An example of DP obfuscation for 2D convolution in TIR form.

DP transformation has extended the loop over output channels by introducing

process.

The end-to-end execution ﬂow begins with model im-
port and high-level relay rewriting, proceeds through metadata
propagation and operator lowering, and culminates in backend code generation with embedded obfuscation logic.

At the
operator level, attribute obfuscation techniques, such as DP,
KE, BS, and HA, are implemented through a combination
of relay rewriting, TE-level operator extension, and low-level
schedule manipulation.

These transformations jointly produce
semantically equivalent yet structurally diversiﬁed operators,
and are designed to persist through TIR lowering and backend
code generation.

For example, DP increases the output channel
dimensionality by appending redundant ﬁlters that lie in the null
space of a projection operator, followed by an explicit projection
that recovers the original output shape.

KE enlarges the convolution kernel by symmetric zero-padding and adjusts the input
accordingly to preserve output equivalence.

These transformations modify the operator’s loop structures and memory access
patterns, as illustrated in Figs. 4 and 5.

Due to space constraints,
we illustrate only the TIR-level structural differences resulting
from DP and KE.

The full operator attribute obfuscation pipeline
and its integration into the compilation process are presented
in Algorithm 1.

At the memory level, layout perturbation is
achieved through two complementary mechanisms.

The full operator attribute obfuscation pipeline

At the memory level, layout perturbation is
achieved through two complementary mechanisms.

MAPO in-
troduces index permutations by altering loop access patterns,
and CAP shifts buffer base addresses by runtime-determined offsets.

These memory-level changes are coordinated through the
memory planning phase and concretely injected into the TIR via
dynamic indexing logic, bound checks, and modiﬁed allocation
calls.

Finally, dummy branching obfuscation is implemented
through CRE, which injects runtime-dependent control ﬂow and
data paths at both IR stages.

Speciﬁcally, Relay passes insert
conditional subgraphs with neutral arithmetic, while TIR-level
modiﬁcations introduce dummy operators using randomized
or input-derived index expressions, ensuring execution traces
diverge across runs while leaving output unchanged.

Together,

these tightly integrated components constitute the core im-
plementation of MACO, delivering a coherent execution ﬂow
that propagates structural obfuscation from high-level graph
transformations down to low-level memory access patterns,
while remaining compatible with TVM’s optimization and code
generation pipeline.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation

content:
To balance security gains against computational overhead,
our framework employs a dual-level control mechanism that
regulates both the frequency and the combination of obfuscation
strategies applied to a deep learning computation graph.

Rather
than uniformly modifying all eligible operators with every
available technique, our mechanism makes two key decisions.
(1) For each obfuscation strategy, determine the fraction of
eligible operators to be transformed. (2) Based on a user-selected
obfuscation tier, choose which subsets of strategies to apply.

A global intensity parameter, denoted as κ ∈[0, 1], governs
the degree of transformation for each strategy.

For every obfuscation technique i (e.g., DP, BS, MAPO, CAP, CO, HA, and SD),
we deﬁne a selection probability function pi(κ) that determines
the proportion of eligible operators to be modiﬁed.

Additionally,
we design a scaling function fi(κ) to adjust strategy-speciﬁc
parameters (such as the expansion factor in DP).

For example,
for DP, the number of output channels under the control of κ is
computed as
j′ = j + ⌊(α −1) · κ · j⌋
(12)
thereby allowing the modiﬁcation strength to scale with κ.

In addition, we introduce a second global parameter, η, which
is used exclusively for controlling the CRE strategy.

Given that
CRE is the most computationally intensive method, it is applied
only at the highest obfuscation tier.

Rather than adjusting the
magnitude of each dummy operation, which remains ﬁxed at
a very small constant ϵ ≪1 to ensure neutrality, η speciﬁes
the fraction of eligible nodes at which a redundant branch is
injected.

injected.

approximately η × N extra dummy operators will be added to

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
This provides a direct knob to control the overall
additional operator count.

We further partition the overall ob-
fuscation conﬁguration into four distinct tiers:
r Tier I: Conservative Obfuscation.

Only low-overhead
strategies, such as MAPO and CAP, are enabled.
r Tier II: Moderate Obfuscation.

In addition to Tier I,
modest-cost strategies like DP and BS are applied.
r Tier III: Enhanced Obfuscation.

Tier II is augmented with
identity-based obfuscation techniques such as CO and HA.
r Tier IV: Comprehensive Obfuscation.

All strategies, in-
cluding the computationally heavy CRE, are activated.

The tier selection is implemented by deﬁning a mapping
from the desired obfuscation level to a subset of strategies.

At the implementation process, both κ and η are embedded
as metadata within the relay IR.

During the IR transformation phase, each obfuscation pass consults these parameters.

A strategy is applied to an eligible operator with probability
pi(κ), and its conﬁguration is scaled by fi(κ).

For CRE, the
overall number of dummy branches inserted is controlled by
η, ensuring that the additional operators comprise a predetermined fraction of the eligible set.

These modiﬁcations propagate
through to the TIR generation phase, where dynamic index ad-
justments and modiﬁed loop bounds reﬂect the intended obfuscation intensity.

This two-level control mechanism thus provides
a systematic and conﬁgurable trade-off between security and
performance.

Execution Flow of Obfuscation Intensity Control: Algorithm 2 outlines our uniﬁed strategy for selecting and applying
obfuscation transformations based on the global parameters
κ and η, and on the chosen obfuscation tier.

These modiﬁcations propagate

justments and modiﬁed loop bounds reﬂect the intended obfuscation intensity.

This two-level control mechanism thus provides
a systematic and conﬁgurable trade-off between security and
performance.

Execution Flow of Obfuscation Intensity Control: Algorithm 2 outlines our uniﬁed strategy for selecting and applying
obfuscation transformations based on the global parameters
κ and η, and on the chosen obfuscation tier.

Note that the
ﬁnal output of the transformation pipeline is a fully obfuscated TIR module M ′, which incorporates both high-level relay
modiﬁcations and low-level scheduling perturbations.

For better
understanding, we also illustrate this process in Fig. 6.

IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON DEPENDABLE AND SECURE COMPUTING, VOL. 22, NO. 6, NOVEMBER/DECEMBER 2025
Detailed obfuscation intensity control ﬂow.

Starting from the original
DL model, we obtain a relay IR in TVM.

We then choose an obfuscation
tier (I-IV) and set the global parameters κ and η.

According to the tier, a
subset of strategies S is determined, and for each strategy i ∈S, each eligible
operator is transformed with probability pi(κ), scaling parameters by fi(κ).

Tier IV additionally injects CRE-based dummy branches (η controls the fraction
of nodes).

All metadata and transformations are embedded in TIR lowering,
producing the ﬁnal obfuscated binary.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
Our goal is to comprehensively assess the security effectiveness, performance overhead, functional correctness, and ad-
vancement of MACO in defending against model extraction and
reverse-engineeringattacks.

InSectionVI-B,wedescribetheexperimental setup, including datasets, model architectures, metrics, and baselines.

Then, Sections VI-C, VI-D, VI-E, and VI-F
detail our ﬁndings for each research question.

A.

Enviroments
Our MACO framework is primarily implemented in C++ and
Python, integrating directly with the TVM compilation pipeline.

The current prototype spans approximately 5.2 K lines of code
(LOC), including custom relay passes for operator transformations, TIR-level obfuscation logic, and auxiliary scripts for
compiler conﬁguration and metadata management.

In the experiments reported below, we target 64-bit ELF
executable ﬁles generated by TVM, focusing on the x86-64
architecture.

We tested MACO on a 64-bit Ubuntu 20.04 system,
running on an AMD Ryzen 7 5800X at 3.80 GHz with 32 GB
of memory, paired with an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 for
GPU-based trials.

We used TVMv0.8, compiled with LLVM11
as the backend.

For the disassembly and low-level binary inspection required
by reverse extraction attacks, we employed IDA Pro 7.5 in
combination with IDAPython scripting to analyze how our
obfuscated binaries appear under static analysis.

Additionally,
to accommodate speciﬁc side-channel attack conﬁgurations, we
incorporated performance proﬁling and custom logging hooks
within the system runtime to capture side-channel traces across
different obfuscation tiers.

B.

of MACO, we employ a diverse set of deep learning
models primarily sourced from the ONNX Model Zoo [23].

These pretrained models already provide complete parameters

Speciﬁcally, we se-

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
Additionally, we include DenseNet121 [26]
and Xception [27] to introduce more complex architectures with
intricate skip connections or depthwise separable convolutions,
thereby posing non-trivial challenges for obfuscation.

Finally,
we deliberately add MobileNetV2 [28] and SqueezeNet [29],
lightweight, optimized networks often deployed on resourceconstrained devices, to highlight MACO’s applicability to models demanding efﬁciency in both size and speed.

These models span a broad range of convolutional network designs, from relatively simple macro-architectures (like
VGG16) to modern, highly optimized or deeper backbones (like
LI et al.: MACO: MODEL ANTI-EXTRACTION VIA COMPILER OBFUSCATION
Xception).

They encompass various channel structures, skipconnection patterns, and convolution variants.

By evaluating
MACO’s obfuscation strategies on both standard and advanced
CNNs, we gain insight into MACO’s robustness and generality
across different complexity levels.

InlinewithcommonusecasesforONNX-baseddeployments,
we compile each model into binary ﬁle through TVM’s pipeline.

We apply MACO transformations at both the relay and TIR
levels, examining metrics such as obfuscation strength, runtime
overhead, and retained accuracy in subsequent experiments.
2) Metrics: We use both security and system performance
metrics.

Security Metrics: The evaluation of security in our approach
is based on three key metrics.

Normalized graph edit distance
(nGED) is used to indicate the obfuscation effect on high-level
graph structures.

Operator attribute recovery accuracy (OARA)
quantiﬁes the percentage of operator attributes (e.g., kernel
size, number of output channels) correctly identiﬁed by the
attacker compared to the ground truth from the original model.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
Security Metrics: The evaluation of security in our approach
is based on three key metrics.

Normalized graph edit distance
(nGED) is used to indicate the obfuscation effect on high-level
graph structures.

Operator attribute recovery accuracy (OARA)
quantiﬁes the percentage of operator attributes (e.g., kernel
size, number of output channels) correctly identiﬁed by the
attacker compared to the ground truth from the original model.

An attribute is counted as correctly recovered when all of its
associated ﬁelds exactly match the original operator.

Operator
connectivity accuracy (OCA) assesses the percentage of topological connections that the attacker correctly reconstructs.

A
connection is considered correct only if the source and target
operators, along with their edge direction, precisely match the
original dataﬂow graph, partial matches or misaligned branches
are treated as errors.

System Performance Metrics: The performance evaluation of
our system is based on three primary metrics.

Latency (ms)
measures the time required to execute a single inference (batch
size = 1) after compilation with TVM at optimization level
-O0, reﬂecting the raw computational cost.

Memory Overhead
quantiﬁes the relative increase in peak memory usage compared
to an unobfuscated (vanilla) baseline.

Output consistency represents the percentage of inference instances where the obfuscated
model produces the identical output label as the original model.

source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf

content:
Latency (ms)
measures the time required to execute a single inference (batch
size = 1) after compilation with TVM at optimization level
-O0, reﬂecting the raw computational cost.

Memory Overhead
quantiﬁes the relative increase in peak memory usage compared
to an unobfuscated (vanilla) baseline.

Output consistency represents the percentage of inference instances where the obfuscated
model produces the identical output label as the original model.

In addition, we enable a suite of transformations (DP, BS, CO,
HA, SD, KE, MAPO, CAP, and CRE) at the relay and TIR levels
based on the tier level, as described in Section III of the paper.
3) Baseline: We evaluate MACO against three representative attack methodologies drawn from prior works, BTD [6],
DS [4], and REDLC [7], which collectively span symbolic
execution-based, side-channel, and DL-driven model extraction
strategies.

In addition, these methods target different hardware
backends.

BTD and REDLC are executed on CPU platforms,
whereas DS operates on GPUs.

To further compare MACO’s
effectiveness with domain-leading defenses, we also include
two SOTA compiler-based protection frameworks in our qualitative baseline.

NNReArch [9] is a tensor scheduling solution
against reverse-engineering.

And SecureTVM [8] is a selective
privacy-preserving compiler for TVM.

We choose these systems
as they represent cutting-edge approaches to safeguarding neural
networks at the compilation level.

C.

Effectiveness Against Model Extraction Attack
1) Measuring Graph Topology Divergence: We ﬁrst design
a controlled experiment to quantitatively assess how much our
Normalized Graph Edit Distance (nGED) between the original and ob-
fuscated networks under Tiers I–IV.

where nodes represent operators with attributes such as kernel

We then compute an approximate graph edit distance

into G′.

Here we focus purely

Results: Fig. 7 plots the normalized GED values for the eight
models under Tiers I-IV.

As expected, Tier I barely changes
the DAG structure, yielding minimal nGED (around 0.01-0.04).

Tier II causes a more pronounced rise (to 0.14-0.21 in some
cases) due to splitting (BS) and channel expansions (DP).

Tier III
further increases nGED, reﬂecting additional node insertions
from CO/HA and kernel padding from KE.

Finally, Tier IV
shows the largest topological gap (up to around 0.50 or more)
in certain architectures such as DenseNet121 and Xception,
underscoring the disruptive effect of CRE’s dummy branches
and conditional paths.
2) Impact on Attacks: In this section, we investigate the
extent to which MACO’s multi-tier obfuscation framework hinders four representative attack methods, BTD [6], DND [5],
DS [4], and REDLC [7].

shows the largest topological gap (up to around 0.50 or more)

2) Impact on Attacks: In this section, we investigate the
extent to which MACO’s multi-tier obfuscation framework hinders four representative attack methods, BTD [6], DND [5],
DS [4], and REDLC [7].

Each of these attacks targets different dimensions of reverse-engineering, from symbolic execution and dynamic analysis to side-channel inference and DL–
based extraction.

We evaluate MACO’s effectiveness using two
metrics, OARA and OCA.

We run each attack on eight commonly used networks and compare results for Tier I, Tier II,

Tier I Setup and Results: Tier I includes only the low-overhead
transformations MAPO, conﬁgured to reorder 20% of memory

We use a global parameter κ = 0.3

networks.

Tier I Setup and Results: Tier I includes only the low-overhead
transformations MAPO, conﬁgured to reorder 20% of memory
accesses randomly.

CAP introduces a random alignment offset
within a range of 16-64 bytes.

We use a global parameter κ = 0.3
to indicate relatively conservative obfuscation.

Table I shows the
OARA and OCA for each of the four attacks across the eight
networks.

We observe that as MAPO and CAP mainly affect
memory layout and cache alignment, they have only a moderate
inﬂuence on BTD and DND’s attribute inferences, reducing
OARAbyaround2-10%.

Ontheotherhand,DSsuffersaslightly
larger drop (often up to 20%), because DS relies on monitoring
memory access features.

At this stage, REDLC shows virtually
no drop since its extraction mechanism does not rely on signals
such as memory alignment.

Tier I does not modify the graph
topology or operator connections, so all four attacks still achieve
relatively high connection recovery.

Tier II Setup and Results: Tier II includes MAPO and CAP
(same as Tier I), and DP with expansion factor α = 1.3, BS with
partition ratio β = 0.4, SD injecting redundant add-sub operations.

We set the global parameter κ = 0.5 to reﬂect more ex-
tensive modiﬁcations than Tier I.

These newly introduced transformations alter operator shapes (DP), split or fuse dataﬂows
(BS), and insert small no-op branches (SD), thus potentially
confusingattributeinferenceacross abroader rangeof operators.

The results are shown in Table II.

(BS), and insert small no-op branches (SD), thus potentially

Compared with Tier I, all
four attacks, including REDLC, now experience a noticeable
drop in OARA, as DP and BS complicate the identiﬁcation of
true operator parameters.

BTD and DND degrade by around
15-30% (depending on network complexity), while DS drops to
the 35-50% range.

REDLC also declines, typically to 75-90%,
indicating that the newly introduced structural modiﬁcations
hamper purely DL-based reconstruction as well.

Table III presents the Tier III results.

Relative to Tier II,

whereas OCA now decreases more noticeably.

Speciﬁcally, the
extra dummy arithmetic in CO and the deeper structure from

As a result, typical OARA values
for DS, for example, dip below 15% on more complex networks
such as DenseNet121 or Xception.

On the other hand, with HA
adding new identity layers and SD continuing to inject branches,
many attacks experience confusion in identifying true layer-to-
layer connectivity.

OCA can fall to around 33.3% or even lower
for particularly branched architectures like DenseNet121 and
Xception.

content:
OCA can fall to around 33.3% or even lower
for particularly branched architectures like DenseNet121 and
Xception.

Tier IV Setup and Results: Tier IV incorporates all transformations from Tiers I–III and additionally enables CRE.

We ﬁx
κ = 1.0.

And a separate global parameter η = 0.6 controlling
the fraction of eligible operators where CRE dummy branches
are injected.

During TIR lowering, CRE introduces conditional
or random-index dummy paths, which are numerically neutral
but add considerable extraneous computations and unplanned
memory accesses.

As shown in Table IV, Tier IV drives OARA
down by another 50% compared to Tier III, and the changes in
OCA likewise reﬂect the disruptive impact of CRE.

Empirically,
LI et al.: MACO: MODEL ANTI-EXTRACTION VIA COMPILER OBFUSCATION
TABLE V
CPU PERFORMANCE: INFERENCE LATENCY (MS) AND MEMORY OVERHEAD (%)
we observe that OARA can drop below 30%, and in certain cases
even fall under 5%.

For the DS attack on Xception, the OARA
stands at 5.1%, marking an 85.8% reduction compared to its
original value of 90.9% prior to obfuscation.

The OCA results
are similarly encouraging, the random control ﬂow illusions
created by CRE add spurious branches or merged paths, causing
attackers to misidentify which sub-branches are part of the ‘real’
computation.

Consequently, for most representative networks,
the ﬁnal OCA often remains below 10%, in the best-case scenario, the obfuscation rate reaches an impressive 99%.

In summary, Tier IV yields the highest level of confusion
for both attributes and connectivity.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
The OCA results
are similarly encouraging, the random control ﬂow illusions
created by CRE add spurious branches or merged paths, causing
attackers to misidentify which sub-branches are part of the ‘real’
computation.

Consequently, for most representative networks,
the ﬁnal OCA often remains below 10%, in the best-case scenario, the obfuscation rate reaches an impressive 99%.

In summary, Tier IV yields the highest level of confusion
for both attributes and connectivity.

Although OARA sees only
incremental changes over Tier III, OCA suffers notably larger
reductions due to the conditional dummy branches introduced
by CRE.

Across Tiers I to IV, MACO progressively degrades
attackers’ ability to OARA and OCA.

Tier I primarily disrupts
side-channel–based inferences of operator parameters (most no-
tably affecting DS), while Tiers II and III introduce fundamental
shape and structural obfuscations that impact all four attacks.

Finally, Tier IV adds dynamic dummy branching (CRE), further
confusing connectivity analysis.

We emphasize that even at Tier IV, the functional correctness
of the model remains unaltered.

The overhead of each tier, along
with a deeper analysis of performance trade-offs, is reported in
Section VI-D.

D.

Performance Overhead of MACO
Having established MACO’s efﬁcacy in obfuscating model
structures, we evaluate the performance overhead it introduces
at different tiers of obfuscation.

In particular, we measure two
metrics on both CPU and GPU platforms.
r Latency (ms): The time required to run a single inference
(batch size = 1) after compilation with TVM at optimization level -O0.

This reﬂects the raw execution cost.
r Memory Overhead (%): The relative increase in peak memory usage compared to an unobfuscated (Vanilla) baseline.

We run single-image inferences on the same eight networks
from our prior evaluation under ﬁve conﬁgurations.

Vanilla
TVM (no obfuscation), and tiers I–IV of MACO.

We compile
each model using TVM at level-O0 to avoid aggressive optimizations that might mask or eliminate obfuscation effects.

For
each model and tier, we record the inference latency in seconds
and the memory overhead relative to the vanilla baseline.

CPU Results: Table V presents the latency and memory
overhead on the CPU.

As shown, Tier I imposes a modest
time penalty (roughly +10% on average) and a small memory
overhead (under +5% in most cases).

With Tier II, execution
times can increase by 30-40% for deeper models (ResNet50 or
DenseNet121), while memory overhead climbs toward +10%.

Tier III sees a 50-70% time overhead and up to +15-20%
memory overhead, primarily due to the insertion of identity
layers and kernel expansions.

Finally, Tier IV has the highest
overhead, reaching 80-90% above baseline, and memory usage
can jump by 20-25% (occasionally more in particularly heavy
architectures like DenseNet121).

content:
Tier III sees a 50-70% time overhead and up to +15-20%
memory overhead, primarily due to the insertion of identity
layers and kernel expansions.

Finally, Tier IV has the highest
overhead, reaching 80-90% above baseline, and memory usage
can jump by 20-25% (occasionally more in particularly heavy
architectures like DenseNet121).

GPU Results: Table VI shows the same eight models running
on an NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU.

As expected, the overhead
trends are similar.

The introduction of each tier progressively
increases runtime, with Tier IV again nearly doubling the latency
in some cases.

Memory overhead also follows a similar pattern,
though the absolute GPU overhead percentages can be slightly
larger for more complex networks, where kernel expansions
(DP/KE) and extra identity layers (HA) cause multiple reconﬁgurations of GPU memory buffers.

Overall, Tier I is lightweight, adding minimal disruptions
at the memory and scheduling levels.

Tiers II-IV progressively increase overheads.

Despite these overheads, we consider
the trade-off justiﬁable in scenarios that require robust antiextraction protection, especially for high-value models deployed
at the edge or in untrusted environments.

E.

Output Consistency
Having analyzed MACO’s obfuscation efﬁcacy and its as-
sociated overhead, we next address the question of accuracy
preservation.

Speciﬁcally, we evaluate whether the obfuscated
networksproducedbyMACOyieldinferenceoutputsthataresemantically and numerically consistent with their original counterparts.

To this end, we compile each of our eight models under
all four obfuscation tiers in TVM, and conduct inference on the
same controlled test inputs for both the obfuscated and vanilla
versions, without any retraining or parameter modiﬁcation.

IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON DEPENDABLE AND SECURE COMPUTING, VOL. 22, NO. 6, NOVEMBER/DECEMBER 2025
TABLE VI
GPU PERFORMANCE: INFERENCE LATENCY (MS) AND MEMORY OVERHEAD (%)
TABLE VII
OUTPUT CONSISTENCY METRICS UNDER MACO OBFUSCATION
To comprehensively assess consistency, we adopt three complementary metrics.

First, we measure the top-1 label match rate,
deﬁned as the percentage of test cases where the top-1 classiﬁca-
tionresultfromtheobfuscatedmodelmatchesthatoftheoriginal
model.

This directly reﬂects semantic equivalence at the output
level.

Second, we compute the maximum absolute deviation
between the output tensors of the original and obfuscated models
(prior to softmax), which captures the largest element-wise
numeric deviation introduced by obfuscation.

Third, we evaluate
prediction conﬁdence deviation, deﬁned as the maximum absolute difference in softmax output probabilities across all classes.

This metric detects any perturbation to the predicted conﬁdence
scores, even when the top-1 class remains unchanged.

Our experiments use a mix of real and randomly generated
inputs that cover representative tensor shapes and value ranges.

All metrics are aggregated over 500 inference trials per model
per tier.

paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
Third, we evaluate
prediction conﬁdence deviation, deﬁned as the maximum absolute difference in softmax output probabilities across all classes.

This metric detects any perturbation to the predicted conﬁdence
scores, even when the top-1 class remains unchanged.

Our experiments use a mix of real and randomly generated
inputs that cover representative tensor shapes and value ranges.

All metrics are aggregated over 500 inference trials per model
per tier.

The results, summarized in Table VII, demonstrate that
MACO preserves 100% top-1 accuracy across all models and
tiers.

Moreover, the observed numeric deviations remain within
machine-precision bounds, less than 10−6 for absolute error and
less than 10−5 for conﬁdence deviation, which are in line with
accepted thresholds in compiler-level correctness testing.

These
results afﬁrm MACO’s design principle while bitwise identity
is not guaranteed due to dynamic branching and ﬂoating-point
reordering, the essential computation semantics and prediction
behavior remain unaffected by obfuscation.

F.

Comparison
In our ﬁnal research question, we provide a qualitative
comparison of MACO against two notable compiler-based de-
fense strategies from the literature, NNReArch [9] and Se-
cureTVM [8].

Since both approaches remain closed-source and
TABLE VIII
COMPARISON WITH THE SOTA
cannot be directly benchmarked with our pipeline, we focus on
the high-level design features they implement, contrasting them
with MACO’s multi-level obfuscation methods.

As summarized in Table VIII, NNReArch primarily investigates the correlation between low-level tensor programs and
signals extracted from side-channel attacks.

However, due to its
lack of protection for operator connection edges and attributes,
it remains partially vulnerable to side-channel or memorytrace-based detection, which can still reveal structural hints.

content:
Since both approaches remain closed-source and
TABLE VIII
COMPARISON WITH THE SOTA
cannot be directly benchmarked with our pipeline, we focus on
the high-level design features they implement, contrasting them
with MACO’s multi-level obfuscation methods.

As summarized in Table VIII, NNReArch primarily investigates the correlation between low-level tensor programs and
signals extracted from side-channel attacks.

However, due to its
lack of protection for operator connection edges and attributes,
it remains partially vulnerable to side-channel or memorytrace-based detection, which can still reveal structural hints.

SecureTVM focuses on secure parameter processing, often
leveraging trusted execution environments.

While this ensures
strong conﬁdentiality for model parameters, it does not ob-
fuscate operator attributes or connection paths in a way that
effectively prevents architecture reverse engineering.

Although
SecureTVM is integrated at the TVM level, its primary focus is
data conﬁdentiality rather than operator or topology obfuscation,
and it incurs notable runtime overhead.

In contrast, MACO offers a diverse set of obfuscation techniques, including attribute expansions, memory-level perturbations, and dummy branching.

This comprehensive approach
strengthens resilience against static, dynamic, and side-channelbased reverse engineering attempts, while allowing customizable overhead control.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
A.

Framework Level or Compiler Stack Level?

While certain obfuscation transformations, such as injecting dummy branches or applying mathematically equivalent
rewrites, can in theory be scripted at the framework level
LI et al.: MACO: MODEL ANTI-EXTRACTION VIA COMPILER OBFUSCATION
(e.g., in PyTorch), implementing them at the compiler level
offers stronger guarantees and broader coverage.

Compilerbased obfuscation operates on the intermediate representation
(IR), enabling uniﬁed transformations across all operators.

It
ensures that obfuscation is preserved through lowering and
code generation, rather than being discarded or optimized away
by downstream passes.

Moreover, only at the compiler level
can transformations precisely target memory layout, scheduling
behavior, and low-level control ﬂow-dimensions inaccessible
from high-level modeling frameworks.

This integration makes
MACO robust against both static and dynamic extraction, and
avoids entangling obfuscation logic with model training or up-
stream toolchains.

B.

Limitations
CompilationPipelineConstraints:MACO’spassesaretightly
interwoven with TVM’s Relay and TIR structures, porting these
passes to other compilers or frameworks would require re-
engineering the transformations.

While many principles would
carry over, the implementation details heavily depend on TVM’s
pass infrastructure and scheduling APIs.

That said, most core
transformations in MACO are conceptually backend-agnostic
and could be re-implemented within other compiler frameworks
that expose comparable intermediate representations.

Achieving
full portability would require adapting to each compiler’s transformation granularity, pass orchestration, and backend-speciﬁc
optimization pipeline, which we leave as promising future
work.

Dynamic Compilation Support: MACO currently assumes
that the network’s computation graph is known at compile time
(as is typical in TVM’s ahead-of-time compilation).

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
That said, most core
transformations in MACO are conceptually backend-agnostic
and could be re-implemented within other compiler frameworks
that expose comparable intermediate representations.

Achieving
full portability would require adapting to each compiler’s transformation granularity, pass orchestration, and backend-speciﬁc
optimization pipeline, which we leave as promising future
work.

Dynamic Compilation Support: MACO currently assumes
that the network’s computation graph is known at compile time
(as is typical in TVM’s ahead-of-time compilation).

For highly
dynamic models or runtime shape changes, additional logic
would be needed to keep obfuscation effective without causing
functional errors.

This limitation is particularly relevant when
considering the applicability of MACO to non-convolutional
architectures such as Transformers and large language models,
where input-dependent control ﬂow, dynamic sequence lengths,
and attention-based token interactions pose new challenges.

Adapting MACO to support such models would necessitate integration with dynamic shape-aware passes and backend-speciﬁc
scheduling schemes, which we leave for future exploration.

Functional versus Bitwise Equivalence: MACO is designed
to maintain functional (mathematical) equivalence, not strict
bitwise identity.

Transformations like expression rewriting or
memory reordering can alter the sequence of ﬂoating-point
operations.

Consequently, outputs may differ at the bit level
due to ﬂoating-point non-associativity, a well-accepted characteristic in modern compilers (e.g., TVM, LLVM) that do not
ensure bitwise determinism.

Our validation therefore focuses
on preserving semantic correctness and numerical stability,
aligning with our goal of semantic obfuscation over deterministic reproducibility.

C.

Potential Extensions
Scheduling Integration: Future work can explore synergy between MACO’s obfuscation passes and advanced
auto-schedulers.

Consequently, outputs may differ at the bit level
due to ﬂoating-point non-associativity, a well-accepted characteristic in modern compilers (e.g., TVM, LLVM) that do not
ensure bitwise determinism.

Our validation therefore focuses
on preserving semantic correctness and numerical stability,
aligning with our goal of semantic obfuscation over deterministic reproducibility.

C.

Potential Extensions
Scheduling Integration: Future work can explore synergy between MACO’s obfuscation passes and advanced
auto-schedulers.

For instance, specialized cost models might
preserve obfuscation transformations while still achieving nearoptimal performance.

Integration of Hardware-Speciﬁc Features: On specialized
hardware backends (e.g., NPUs, ASICs), new obfuscation techniques, such as rearranging on-chip buffer usage, might extend
MACO’s approach beyond CPU/GPU architectures.

paper_title: MACO Model Anti Extraction via Compiler Obfuscation
source_file: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation.pdf
paper_id: MACO_Model_Anti-Extraction_via_Compiler_Obfuscation
content:
MACO presents a novel multi-tier approach to compiler-level
obfuscation for DNN, focusing on topology and execution trace
confusion.

It is complementary to encryption or enclave-based
methods that protect the model weights.

By offering tunable
levels of obfuscation (Tiers I-IV), MACO covers a wide range
of deployment scenarios, from lightweight memory reordering
to comprehensive, branching-heavy transformations.

The ex-
perimental results show that MACO achieves strong resilience
against reverse-engineering attacks without compromising the
model’s functional correctness.

paper_title: Codellm Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
source_file: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing.pdf
paper_id: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
content:
Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
Code LLMs with Program Analysis Insights
Rahul Krishna∗†1, Rangeet Pan∗†2, Raju Pavuluri3†, Srikanth Tamilselvam4‡, Maja Vukovic5†, and Saurabh Sinha6†
†IBM Research, Yorktown Heights, NY 10598, USA.
‡IBM Research, Bangalore, KA 560045, India.

Abstract—Large Language Models for Code (or code LLMs)
are increasingly gaining popularity and capabilities, offering a
wide array of functionalities such as code completion, code generation, code summarization, test generation, code translation, and
more.

To leverage code LLMs to their full potential, developers
must provide code-specific contextual information to the models.

These are typically derived and distilled using program analysis
tools.

However, there exists a significant gap—these static analysis
tools are often language-specific and come with a steep learning
curve, making their effective use challenging.

These tools are
tailored to specific program languages, requiring developers to
learn and manage multiple tools to cover various aspects of the
their code base.

Moreover, the complexity of configuring and
integrating these tools into the existing development environments
add an additional layer of difficulty.

This challenge limits the
potential benefits that could be gained from more widespread
and effective use of static analysis in conjunction with LLMs.

To address this challenge, we present codellm-devkit (hereafter, CLDK), an open-source library that significantly simplifies
the process of performing program analysis at various levels of
granularity for different programming languages to support code
LLM use cases.

As a Python library, CLDK offers developers
an intuitive and user-friendly interface, making it incredibly
easy to provide rich program analysis context to code LLMs.

source_file: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing.pdf
paper_id: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
content:
To address this challenge, we present codellm-devkit (hereafter, CLDK), an open-source library that significantly simplifies
the process of performing program analysis at various levels of
granularity for different programming languages to support code
LLM use cases.

As a Python library, CLDK offers developers
an intuitive and user-friendly interface, making it incredibly
easy to provide rich program analysis context to code LLMs.

With this library, developers can effortlessly integrate detailed,
code-specific insights that enhance the operational efficiency and
effectiveness of LLMs in coding tasks.

CLDK is available as an
open-source library at https://github.com/IBM/codellm-devkit.

paper_title: Codellm Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
source_file: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing.pdf
paper_id: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
content:
The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) has accelerated the use of insightful artificial intelligence in software
development workflows.

This has enabled a wide array of
capabilities such as code completion [1], [2], generation [3]–
[5], summarization [6]–[8], test generation [9], [10], program
repair [11], [12], refactoring [12], and translation [13].

These
language models have the potential to improve developer productivity and code quality significantly by automating routine
tasks and providing intelligent insights.

To fully leverage the capabilities of LLMs for software
engineering (SE) tasks, it is critical to provide them with
rich, code-specific contextual information, which can be de-
rived from program analysis tools that perform static and/or
dynamic analysis on the codebases.

These analysis tools can
∗Equal Contribution
Email: {1rkrsn, 2 rangeet.pan}@ibm.com, {3pavuluri, 5maja, 6sinhas}@us.ibm.com,
* each tool is implemented 
    in a different language
software systems.
identify code patterns, dependencies, and potential issues that,
when supplied to code LLMs, can enhance their contextual
understanding of large codebases (beyond what was seen in
their pre-training dataset) thereby impacting their overall performance, accuracy, and, as a consequence, their usefulness.

Applying program analysis tools in the context of LLMs,
however, presents several challenges: (1) most existing static
analysis tools are highly specialized for individual programming languages, each with their own expressive capabilities
and idiosyncrasies, and further (2) existing tools like WALA
(for Java), LLVM (for Rust, and other C-family of languages),
and CodeQL each adopt distinct internal representations, ab-
straction levels, and querying mechanisms making it hard
to generalize across language families.

Applying program analysis tools in the context of LLMs,
however, presents several challenges: (1) most existing static
analysis tools are highly specialized for individual programming languages, each with their own expressive capabilities
and idiosyncrasies, and further (2) existing tools like WALA
(for Java), LLVM (for Rust, and other C-family of languages),
and CodeQL each adopt distinct internal representations, ab-
straction levels, and querying mechanisms making it hard
to generalize across language families.

As a result, enabling
program analysis across multiple languages within a project
introduces a number of unique challenges that arise not merely

due to the diversity of languages, but also because of the differences in how these analysis tools model the code structure,
the inherent semantic relationships, and their behavior.

For developers, this means grappling with different APIs,
data formats, and tooling conventions.

The complexity arises
from having to understand the specific design choices un-
derpinning each tool—ranging from abstract syntax trees
to intermediate representations and proprietary query languages—rather than simply managing tools for multiple languages.

Further, integrating these tools into development environments often requires custom configurations and adaptations
that further complicate the process, leading to significant
overhead and limiting their usability.

This work offers codellm-devkit (hereafter, CLDK), an
open-source Python-based library1 designed to simplify the
process of performing program analysis at various levels
of granularity.

CLDK provides an intuitive and user-friendly
interface that abstracts the complexities of traditional static
analysis tools.

Further, integrating these tools into development environments often requires custom configurations and adaptations
that further complicate the process, leading to significant
overhead and limiting their usability.

This work offers codellm-devkit (hereafter, CLDK), an
open-source Python-based library1 designed to simplify the
process of performing program analysis at various levels
of granularity.

CLDK provides an intuitive and user-friendly
interface that abstracts the complexities of traditional static
analysis tools.

By supporting multiple programming languages
and offering easy integration capabilities, CLDK enables de-
velopers to effortlessly extract rich program analysis context
and supply it to code LLMs.

By lowering the barrier to
performing comprehensive program analysis, CLDK aims to
empower developers to harness the full potential of code LLMs
without the overhead of mastering multiple tools or dealing
with complex integrations.

We conjecture that CLDK will contribute significantly to the software engineering community by
facilitating more efficient and effective development practices.

Our contributions in this paper are as follows:
◦Development of CLDK.

We introduce CLDK, detailing its
architecture, features, and how it addresses the identified
challenges by providing a unified and simplified approach to
program analysis.
◦Integration with LLMs.

We demonstrate how CLDK can
be seamlessly integrated with code LLMs to enhance various
coding tasks, such as code generation and repair, by providing
enriched contextual information.
◦Developer Survey.

We interviewed 15 researchers and de-
velopers at IBM to understand their code LLM-based use cases

paper_title: Codellm Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
source_file: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing.pdf
paper_id: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
content:
The construction of CLDK was motivated by a pressing
need to create a unified framework that integrates various tools
required for building LLM-specific code use cases, while providing an abstraction layer that supports multiple programming
languages.

Performing analysis for different programming
languages often requires understanding the intricate details
of various program analysis tools, each of which can have
a steep learning curve.

Furthermore, these tools vary in their
level of support for different languages, and extending analysis
to additional languages often demands significant extra effort
from developers.

To highlight these challenges, Figs. 2 and 3 demonstrate
how different program analysis frameworks require vastly
different approaches to accomplish the same task—that of
having to retrieve all the class names from an application.

Using CodeQL CLI [14], the user needs to create a semmle
query and save it to disk, then run it using the subprocess
call, and then clean up the pipe-separated text output to extract
the desired results.

This process of performing multiple file
i/o operations and execution of subprocess calls (which may
require previlidge access) can be unscalable and may pose
security threats.

Further, performing string splits and strip
can be challenging and error prone.

Alternatively, with Treesitter [15] (see Fig. 3), simpler queries can be written, and
the Python library can be used to parse the raw AST to
retrieve class names.

However, the developer needs to learn
the grammar for each language, and construct new queries
that leverage the language grammar.

This has a steep learning
curve and may not scale in practice.

CLDK simplifies this process
considerably—the developer just needs to instantiate a CLDK

application, and call the get_classes API to extract all class
names from the application.

paper_title: Codellm Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
source_file: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing.pdf
section_title: III. DEVELOPER SURVEY ON TOOL NEEDS
paper_id: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
content:
In this section, we describe a study in which we learn
the overall experience of IBM developers and researchers
in the building, using, and interacting with code LLMs and
their need for systematic program analysis for their use cases.

We conducted an interview of 15 IBM Research employees,
who have been an integral part of the organization’s AI for
Code initiative.

Our main objective is to understand (a) the
overall experience of code LLM, (b) various use cases of code
LLMs, (c) experience with existing program analysis tools,
(d) experience with prompting and other aspect of code LLM
and various available tools, and (e) finally we investigate a
developers’ and researchers’ wishlist on a tool that they would
prefer to use in their workflow.

Cohort Selection.

Our survey involved 15 participants who
actively build and/or use CodeLLMs in enterprise pipelines
at IBM.

The survey cohort represents a balance between
researchers (8 participants) and engineers (7 participants) to
gather diverse insights with varying degrees of experience
(with a median of 15 years of SE and 17½ years of Research
experience see Table I).

We interviewed each participant
over a 30-minute call and recorded the session.

Participants
for whom we exhausted our allocated meeting time or for
whom additional information was needed before answering
our questions, we followed-up with additional sessions.

Questionnaire Design.

We divided the questionnaire into four
categories – (a) The roles and experiences of the participants,
(b) background and demographics of the participants, their
exposure to Code LLMs, and their Code LLM use cases,
(c) challenges pertaining to the use of program analysis
and construction code LLM-based solutions; (d) prompting
and LLM features, where we query the participants’ use of
prompting, RAG, agents and other framework.

A.

source_file: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing.pdf
section_title: III. DEVELOPER SURVEY ON TOOL NEEDS

content:
We divided the questionnaire into four
categories – (a) The roles and experiences of the participants,
(b) background and demographics of the participants, their
exposure to Code LLMs, and their Code LLM use cases,
(c) challenges pertaining to the use of program analysis
and construction code LLM-based solutions; (d) prompting
and LLM features, where we query the participants’ use of
prompting, RAG, agents and other framework.

A.

Interview results
1) Background of solutions with code LLM: Out of 15
participants, we observed a wide variety of designation (A1)
and a large distribution of SE experience (A2) with a minimum
of 3 years to a maximum of 30 years (see Table I).

In terms
of their background in LLMs, we made the following key
observations:
• Rating LLM experience (B1).

A majority of the participants had either high (53%) or medium (33%) exposure to
using LLM with a minority (14%) had low exposure to LLM-
based use cases, respectively.
• Primary LLM use-case (B2).

The majority or our participants mentioned use cases such as code generation, code
explanation, code repair, test generation, code refactoring, and
application modernization.

Two participants indicated their
primary focus was developing new prompting languages.
• The lingua-franca of LLM development (B3).

Nearly all
participants noted that they use Python to develop LLM-based
solutions, while one participant mentioned using TypeScript,
primarily due to having to build plugins for VS Code.
• The target language for LLM development (B4).

Most
code LLM use cases, according to our participants, were
focused on Java followed closely by Python, Go, and
Javascript/Typescript.

• Time to prototyping vs time to productionizing (B7).

Ma-
jority of our interview participants agreed that a prototype
typically takes a week to a month, whereas a full production
take anywhere from 6 months to a year.
• Time spent obtaining program analysis (B8).

Most of
our participants suggested that they spend a medium amount
of their time obtaining analysis insights.

Some engineers
suggested delegating the analysis to subject matter experts on
program analysis to alleviate the learning curve.

Lesson: Most participants had medium to high LLM experience prototyping and building CodeLLM based applications.

Python was their dominant language for development, with
Java as their main target language.

Most used program
analysis tools, either open-source or custom-built, and
spent a significant amount of time obtaining program
analysis insights highlighting that program analysis is both
critical and time-intensive.
2) Need for Better/Easier Program Analysis Tools: In these
questions, we attempt to understand the key challenges users
face when they program analysis in their solution.

Overall, we
identified a few areas that could be improved for the existing
program analysis approaches.
• Program Analysis Depth (C1 and C2).

A key concern
raised was the availability of various tools that can perform
program analysis at different levels of complexity.

Participants
unanimously agreed that many LLM-based use cases require
lighter analysis, which can be efficiently handled by AST-
based tools like Tree-sitter and Antlr.

paper_title: Codellm Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
source_file: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing.pdf
section_title: III. DEVELOPER SURVEY ON TOOL NEEDS
paper_id: Codellm-Devkit: A Framework for Contextualizing
content:
Overall, we
identified a few areas that could be improved for the existing
program analysis approaches.
• Program Analysis Depth (C1 and C2).

A key concern
raised was the availability of various tools that can perform
program analysis at different levels of complexity.

Participants
unanimously agreed that many LLM-based use cases require
lighter analysis, which can be efficiently handled by AST-
based tools like Tree-sitter and Antlr.

However, more complex
analyses necessitate the use of advanced tools such as WALA,
Soot, or LLVM.
• Support for multiple programming languages (C3).

Our participants rated this aspect at an average of 2 (not
very effective) on a scale of 1 (not at all effective) to 5
(very effective).

A major concern was the scattered support
for various languages often means that extending support
for another language requires switching tools and rewriting
analysis code from scratch, which they suggested hampered
their productivity.

Participants agreed unanimously that a tool
with comprehensive support for multiple languages would
significantly enhance productivity and streamline their efforts.
• Learning curve (C4).

On average, all participants rated the
learning curve as high (4) or very high (5) with the lowest rating being 1 (very low).

They indicated that each tool presents
its own learning challenges.

For instance, participants using
WALA acknowledged that, despite its powerful capabilities
for in-depth program analysis, it has a steep learning curve.
• Need for better tools (C5).

When posed with this question,
participants bemoaned the lack of inconsistency among different tools, particularly in terms of analysis precision, flexibility
for custom analysis, output format, required skill level, and
verification of analysis accuracy.

These challenges overlap
with previous discussions on analysis depth and the skills
needed to integrate these tools into a workflow.
